message:
chore(beads): sync issue store

Two-line status touch from `br sync --flush-only`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-ngl.6","title":"Web: Add Sorting and Filtering to Tables","description":"## Problem
Build history and worker tables have no sorting or filtering. With many builds or workers, users can't find what they need quickly. Tables should be interactive.

## Solution
Add to tables:
1. Column headers clickable for sorting (asc/desc)
2. Sort indicator icons
3. Filter input for searching
4. Pagination for large datasets

## Implementation Details
- Use React state for sort column and direction
- Client-side sorting (data already loaded)
- Filter with debounced input
- Visual sort indicators (▲/▼)

## Build History Table Filters
- Status filter (success/failed/all)
- Worker filter (dropdown)
- Date range filter
- Command search

## Workers Table Sorting
- Sort by status (healthy first)
- Sort by slot availability
- Sort by speed score

## Files to Modify
- web/src/components/builds/build-history-table.tsx - add sorting/filtering
- web/src/components/workers/workers-grid.tsx - add sorting
- web/src/components/ui/table.tsx - add sortable header component

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Click column header to sort
- [ ] Sort direction indicator visible
- [ ] Filter input filters table rows
- [ ] Filters persist during polling updates
- [ ] Pagination for >50 rows

 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-ngl.7","title":"Web: WCAG AA Accessibility Compliance","description":"## Problem
Dashboard has accessibility gaps:
- No aria-labels on custom components
- Keyboard navigation not fully tested
- Focus indicators could be stronger
- Truncated content only accessible via hover

## Solution
Audit and fix accessibility issues:
1. Add aria-labels to all interactive elements
2. Ensure all actions keyboard-accessible
3. Add visible focus rings
4. Make truncated content expandable
5. Add skip-to-main link

## Specific Fixes Needed
- Sidebar nav: aria-current for active link
- Refresh buttons: aria-label="Refresh data"
- Progress bars: aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax
- Tables: scope="col" on headers, aria-sort
- Truncated commands: expandable on focus, not just hover
- Color contrast: verify all text meets 4.5:1 ratio

## Testing
- Tab through entire app
- Test with VoiceOver/NVDA
- Run Lighthouse accessibility audit
- Use axe browser extension

## Files to Modify
- web/src/components/layout/sidebar.tsx - aria-current
- web/src/components/ui/button.tsx - aria-label support
- web/src/components/ui/progress.tsx - aria attributes
- web/src/components/ui/table.tsx - scope, aria-sort
- web/src/components/builds/build-history-table.tsx - expandable commands

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Lighthouse accessibility score > 90
- [ ] All interactive elements keyboard accessible
- [ ] Screen reader announces all content
- [ ] Focus visible on all interactive elements
- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1)

 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-ngl.8","title":"Web: Add Dark/Light Mode Toggle","description":"## Problem
Dashboard is dark-mode only. While dark mode is appropriate for developer tools, some users prefer light mode, especially in bright environments. The color scheme exists in globals.css but isn't toggleable.

## Solution
Add theme toggle:
1. Theme toggle button in sidebar footer
2. Persist preference in localStorage
3. Respect system preference initially
4. Smooth transition between themes

## Implementation
Use next-themes package:
```tsx
// web/src/components/providers/theme-provider.tsx
import { ThemeProvider } from 'next-themes'

export function Providers({ children }) {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider attribute="class" defaultTheme="system">
      {children}
    </ThemeProvider>
  )
}

// Toggle button
import { useTheme } from 'next-themes'
const { theme, setTheme } = useTheme()
<Button onClick={() => setTheme(theme === 'dark' ? 'light' : 'dark')}>
  {theme === 'dark' ? <Sun /> : <Moon />}
</Button>
```

## Files to Modify
- package.json - add next-themes
- web/src/app/layout.tsx - wrap with ThemeProvider
- web/src/components/layout/sidebar.tsx - add toggle button
- web/src/app/globals.css - ensure light mode colors defined

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Toggle button visible in sidebar
- [ ] Theme persists across sessions
- [ ] System preference respected initially
- [ ] Smooth transition animation
- [ ] All components work in both themes

-{"id":"remote_compilation_helper-nhrjr","title":"RCH-E100 doesn't distinguish key-missing from host-down from auth-refused","description":"All three SSH failure modes currently emit the same RCH-E100 with the same generic remediation. An auto-recovery agent can only branch on the inner 'Failed to connect' vs 'Permission denied (publickey)' substring, which is fragile.

Suggested fix: subdivide into

- RCH-E100 (TCP unreachable / connect refused / connect timeout)
- RCH-E101 (auth/key failure — currently aliased; keep code or use a sub-class)
- RCH-E102 (host-key mismatch — currently aliased)

The internal SSH probe in rch-common/src/discovery.rs already distinguishes these — surface them in the probe result.

Symptom evidence: css /home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-asupersync/5acd3d96-736d-4309-af27-e69b7a6ff502.jsonl L1059 — the agent had to run a separate `ls ~/.ssh/` to figure out which subcategory it was, then queued a question to the human instead of self-recovering.

Filed-by: rch skill mining run 2026-04-22.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T23:53:31.150599409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T23:53:31.150599409Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["errors","ssh","ux"]}
+{"id":"remote_compilation_helper-nhrjr","title":"RCH-E100 doesn't distinguish key-missing from host-down from auth-refused","description":"`rch workers probe --all` collapses every SSH failure into the human-readable `RCH-E100` bucket in the `error` field, even though the catalog already defines distinct codes for the underlying conditions:

- RCH-E100: SSH connection to worker failed (TCP)
- RCH-E101: SSH authentication failed
- RCH-E102: SSH key not found or invalid format
- RCH-E103: SSH host key verification failed
- RCH-E108: Connection refused
- RCH-E109: TCP connection timed out

The codes exist; the probe pipeline just doesn't surface them in its rendered output, so an auto-recovery agent has to fall back to substring matching on `error` strings (`Permission denied (publickey`, `no such identity`, `Connection refused`, etc.) which is fragile and version-coupled.

Suggested fix in `rchd/src/workers.rs` probe path (or wherever `classify_ssh_error` / `format_ssh_report` lands the result):

1. When constructing `WorkerProbeResult`, also set a structured `error_code: Option<String>` field with the appropriate `RCH-E10x` value derived from the inner error type (the discovery code in `rch-common/src/discovery.rs` already distinguishes them).
2. Update the JSON shape so agents can branch on `.data[].error_code` instead of substring-matching `.data[].error`.
3. The text `error` field can stay for human readability.

Symptom evidence: css /home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-asupersync/5acd3d96-736d-4309-af27-e69b7a6ff502.jsonl L1059 — agent had to run a separate `ls ~/.ssh/` to figure out which subcategory it was in, then queued a question to the human instead of self-recovering. The skill's `scripts/auto_recover.sh` currently substring-matches on `Permission denied (publickey`, `no such identity`, `Connection refused` — a structured `error_code` would let it branch on stable identifiers.

Filed-by: rch skill mining run 2026-04-22; revised 2026-04-22 to reflect that the codes already exist as distinct entries — the bug is that the probe doesn't use them in its output.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T23:53:31.150599409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:07:28.428373512Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["errors","ssh","ux"]}
 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-nmt1","title":"Idea: Artifact integrity verification (blake3)","description":"## Background
RCH relies on rsync for artifact retrieval; silent corruption is unlikely but high impact. Optional integrity checks provide confidence for critical builds.

## Goals
- Optionally verify key artifacts via blake3 hashes post-transfer.
- Keep disabled by default to avoid extra overhead.

## Design / Approach
- Add config: transfer.verify_artifacts = true/false.
- For known artifact patterns, compute blake3 on worker and locally; compare.
- On mismatch, surface actionable error and fall back to local execution on next run.

## Tasks / Subtasks
- Add verification routine to transfer pipeline.
- Determine which artifacts to verify (binaries, test reports).
- Extend mock transport to simulate mismatch.

## Tests
- Unit: hash comparison and failure handling.
- Integration: mock mismatch triggers error message.
- E2E: verification disabled by default.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Integrity verification is optional and reliable.
- Clear error messages on mismatch.

## Logging & E2E
- E2E script: scripts/e2e_bd-377q.sh (one script per bead unless intentionally combined).
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger (tests/true_e2e/logging.rs) or scripts/test_lib.sh log_json.
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result, error (if any).
- Phases to log (as applicable): classify, select, sync_up, exec, sync_down, cleanup, summary.
- Include a final summary block with pass/fail counts and total elapsed time.

## Edge Cases & UX
- Skip verification if artifact missing or exceeds size cap.
- Hash on worker before download to avoid duplicate I/O.
- Mismatch error includes both hashes for debugging.

## E2E Outline
- Mock mismatch -> clear error with hashes.
- Verification disabled -> no extra output.
- Successful verification logged with duration.

## Unit Tests (Detailed)
- rch/src/transfer.rs: hash compute + compare; size cap skip.
- error path includes both hashes and artifact name.

## E2E Script Notes
- scripts/e2e_bd-377q.sh: mock mismatch -> clear error with hashes.
","notes":"## Implementation Complete - Phase 1 (EmeraldMoose)

### Completed:
1. **Config options in TransferConfig** (rch-common/src/types.rs)
   - `verify_artifacts: bool` (default false)
   - `verify_max_size_bytes: u64` (default 100MB)

2. **Artifact verification module** (rch-common/src/artifact_verify.rs)
   - `FileHash` - blake3 hash + size
   - `ArtifactManifest` - file path -> hash mapping
   - `VerificationResult` - passed/failed/skipped lists
   - `VerificationFailure` - detailed mismatch info
   - `compute_file_hash()` - blake3 computation
   - `verify_artifacts()` - verify against manifest
   - `create_manifest()` - create manifest from files
   - Detailed error formatting with actionable suggestions

3. **9 unit tests** - all passing

### Ready for Use:
The utilities can be used programmatically to verify artifacts. Example:
```rust
use rch_common::{create_manifest, verify_artifacts};

// Create manifest before transfer
let manifest = create_manifest(dir, &file_list, worker_id);

// After transfer, verify
let result = verify_artifacts(dir, &manifest, max_size);
if !result.all_passed() {
    eprintln!("{}", result.format_failures());
}
```

### Future Work (Phase 2 - separate bead):
- [ ] Worker-side blake3 computation via SSH
- [ ] Automatic manifest creation after remote build
- [ ] Integration into retrieve_artifacts pipeline
- [ ] E2E test script

### Verification:
- cargo check passes
- All 9 tests pass
- Config defaults verified","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:53:55.418582543Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T05:19:34.814655713Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T05:19:34.814523477Z","external_ref":"bd-377q","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"remote_compilation_helper-nuuqt","title":"rch self-test: enforce upper-bound timeout per worker (no infinite hang)","description":"Multiple agent sessions report `rch self-test` (especially `--all`) hanging indefinitely against one slow/wedged worker. The bd-w5r9 bug touched on this; recent versions take `--timeout` per worker but the default is generous and `--all` mode amplifies any single slow worker.

Suggested fix:

- Cap default per-worker timeout to 180s (override-able via `--timeout`)
- In `--all` mode, run workers in parallel with a fleet-wide deadline
- On timeout, emit a clear RCH-E203 with the worker id and the partial result
- Surface a concise summary at the end ("3 ok, 1 timed out, 0 failed") so the agent can act on partial info

Code home: rch/src/commands/self_test.rs (or similar).

Symptom evidence: filed previously as bd-w5r9 ("self-test command hangs indefinitely with no output"); cass surfaced 30+ hits on "Worker self-test failed" without timeout context.

Filed-by: rch skill mining run 2026-04-22.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T23:54:12.700734479Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T23:54:12.700734479Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["reliability","self-test"]}
+{"id":"remote_compilation_helper-nuuqt","title":"rch self-test: enforce upper-bound timeout per worker (no infinite hang)","description":"Multiple agent sessions report `rch self-test` (especially `--all`) hanging or running indefinitely against one slow/wedged worker. The CLI exposes `--timeout <seconds>` (default 300s per docs) but the empirical evidence is that some `--all` invocations don't surface any output for many minutes, suggesting the timeout is either not enforced consistently or the work scheduler doesn't apply it per-worker.

Suggested fix:

- Audit that `--timeout` is enforced per-worker in `--all` mode (not just on the parent task)
- In `--all` mode, run workers in parallel with a fleet-wide deadline so one slow worker doesn't gate the others
- On per-worker timeout, emit a clear RCH-E203 with the worker id and any partial result rather than silently hanging
- Surface a concise summary at the end ("3 ok, 1 timed out, 0 failed") so the agent can act on partial info even if some workers timed out
- Do NOT lower the default `--timeout` from 300s; that would regress slower-builder workers

Symptom evidence: filed previously as bd-w5r9 ("self-test command hangs indefinitely with no output"); cass surfaced 30+ hits on "Worker self-test failed" without timeout context. Confirmed in this skill-improvement run by trying `rch self-test --all --timeout 120` against a 7-worker fleet — single-worker mode honors the timeout cleanly, but `--all` is harder to reason about.

Filed-by: rch skill mining run 2026-04-22; revised 2026-04-22 to remove regression risk.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T23:54:12.700734479Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:07:28.198260477Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["reliability","self-test"]}

 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-o4z5","title":"Implement robust drift detection in benchmark_scheduler.rs","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T19:09:29.278695018Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T20:09:35.763552330Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T20:09:35.763535068Z","close_reason":"Implemented robust drift detection: added BenchmarkConditions to SpeedScore, compares current telemetry to benchmark-time conditions with weighted composite (CPU 50%, load 30%, memory 20%).
